results.title: How We Built Resyn: A Marketplace for Musicians, by Musicians
results.content:
That was the frustration that led us to build Resyn, a new music gear marketplace designed by and for musicians. Resyn officially launched in July 2025, but the idea started long before — rooted in late-night conversations, countless frustrations with existing platforms, and a shared vision that buying, selling, and trading gear could feel different.

## The Problem We Saw

If you’ve ever sold gear online, you probably know the pain points:

Most existing platforms weren’t designed for players. They were designed for transactions. That distinction matters. As musicians ourselves, we wanted a space where trust, safety, and community came first — where buying a guitar or trading a synth didn’t just feel like e-commerce, but like joining a conversation.

## Building Something Different [...] ## Building Something Different

From day one, our mission with Resyn was clear: build a player-first marketplace that restores the soul of gear exchange. We asked hundreds of musicians what they hated about current platforms, and the feedback was blunt: too expensive, too risky, and too impersonal.

That’s why Resyn launched with five core principles:

These features weren’t afterthoughts — they came straight from musicians telling us what they needed most.

## From Frustration to Launch [...] ## From Frustration to Launch

Resyn’s co-founders, Christopher Stanley and John Targon, come from different but complementary backgrounds. Christopher brings the perspective of a lifelong musician who’s experienced firsthand the friction of existing platforms. John, a designer and entrepreneur with years of experience building global brands, saw how marketplaces lacked the intentionality and curation musicians deserved.

As John puts it: “Gear is currency for musicians. It’s meant to be in motion, not collecting dust. Resyn is about creating a system where gear can move easily and with trust, so artists can focus on making music.”

results.title: Troubleshooting Resin 3D Printing Failures Caused by Improper Support
results.content:
## Final Thoughts

Support breakage is one of the most common causes of failed resin prints—but it can be avoided. By improving support design, fine-tuning exposure and lift settings, and properly orienting your model, you can significantly increase your printing success rate and produce higher-quality prints.

If you're still having trouble, we invite you to join the Siraya Tech Community on Facebook to share your print settings, print results, and get support from fellow users around the world.

Happy printing!

 Opens in a new window. [...] ## Problem Overview: What Does a Failed Support Look Like

If you're experiencing any of the following, support breakage may be the culprit:

 Parts of your model are missing or layers are visibly broken
 Overhangs collapse during printing
 Supports detach and fall into the resin vat
 Critical areas with fine details fail to print properly

These issues often occur in models with overhangs, tall structures, or long-duration prints.

## Root Causes and Solutions

### A. Weak Support Structure Design

Common Issues:

 Too small or thin contact points that detach easily
 Low support density, especially under heavy or long overhangs
 Long, narrow supports that flex or snap during layer separation

# Troubleshooting Resin 3D Printing Failures Caused by Improper Support

3D Resin and Silicone TipsSupport WC

Troubleshooting Resin 3D Printing Failures Caused by Improper Support

Share

0 comments

In resin 3D printing, stable support structures are critical to a successful print. When supports break during the print process, it can lead to collapsed overhangs, missing layers, and incomplete models. This guide will help you identify the signs of failed supports, understand the underlying causes, and implement effective solutions to improve your success rate.

# Troubleshooting Sync Issues: How to Verify and Resolve Device Syncing Problems

Quickly troubleshoot device syncing issues using sync history, ReadiOne, and ReadiSync.

When a device isn't syncing as expected, the first step is to check the sync history. This feature within our product provides detailed information about when a device last synced, helping you quickly determine if a syncing issue exists.

You can also use ReadiOne or ReadiSync to verify the last sync. These tools allow you to search for recent sync activities, ensuring that everything is functioning properly.

ReadiSyncReadiOne [...] ReadiSyncReadiOne

If the sync history or apps show that a sync has occurred recently, then there is no syncing issue. You can confidently move on from syncing concerns and explore other potential issues:

## Device not pairing

## Sleep recording issues

## No ReadiScore

However, if after checking the sync history and using ReadiOne or ReadiSync, you find that there’s no recorded sync, then a syncing issue is confirmed.

To resolve this, follow the appropriate troubleshooting steps based on your specific device and product:

Within these 2 articles, just select the app you want to troubleshoot.

## Syncing issues with a ReadiWatch

## 

## Syncing issues with a ReadiBand [...] ## 

## Syncing issues with a ReadiBand

If none of the troubleshooting steps resolve the issue, you can contact our support team for further assistance. It's important to note that we will need the app logs to investigate this issue further. When you contact support through the app, the logs will be automatically sent to us. However, if you already have the logs (or you received the logs), please send them along with a description of the steps you’ve tried to resolve the issue. For more details, please refer to our article on how to contact support:

## How to contact support
